text: It seems a proposition, which will not admit of much dispute, that all our ideas are nothing but copies of our impressions, or, in other words, that it is impossible for us to _think_ of any thing, which we have not antecedently _felt_, either by our external or internal senses.
author: David Hume
source: Project Gutenberg eBook #9662 (An Enquiry Concerning Human Understanding), plain text
out_pulls_note: Out-pulls the blank-slate gloss: Hume's 'copy principle' (every idea traces to a prior impression) is the active engine of empiricism, sharper than the passive 'mind starts blank' restatement.
search_notes: Gutenberg #9662. Hume restates the copy principle here at the head of Section VII, Part I; an earlier identical statement appears in Section II (§12-13: 'all our ideas... are copies of our impressions'). Chose the VII.I restatement for its crisp standalone form. IMPORTANT: the Gutenberg plain text marks italics with underscores (_think_, _felt_); these underscores are kept in the text so it byte-matches literally — a renderer should strip them for display. Verified verbatim substring after normalization. High confidence. Considered Locke's Essay II.i §2 instead, but reserved that for Tabula Rasa to avoid duplicate source.
